questions.text: Bacillus thuringiensis forms protein crystals that contain insecticidal protein. This protein:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Binds with epithelial cells in the midgut of the insect pest ultimately killing it
options.explanation: Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) produces insecticidal proteins that, when ingested by insect pests, bind to receptors on the midgut epithelial cells, causing damage to the gut lining and ultimately leading to the death of the insect. This is a well-known mechanism of action for Bt proteins, which are widely used in genetically modified crops and insecticides to control insect pests.

options.text: Is coded by several genes including the gene cry
options.explanation: Bt proteins are encoded by specific genes, but it is not typically multiple genes. The proteins are produced by the bacterium and then released.

options.text: Is activated by acid pH of the foregut of the insect pest
options.explanation: Bt proteins do not require an acidic pH to be activated; they are effective once ingested by the insect pest and exposed to the alkaline conditions of the insect's gut.

options.text: Does not kill the carrier bacterium which is itself resistant to this toxin
options.explanation: Bt proteins do not kill the carrier bacterium, as Bt itself is naturally resistant to the toxin it produces.

questions.explanation: Soil bacterium Bacillus thuringiensis produces proteins that kill certain insects like lepidopterans (tobacco budworm, armyworm), coleopterans (beetles), and dipterans (flies, mosquitoes). B. thuringiensis forms some protein crystals which contain a toxic insecticidal protein. This toxin does not kill the Bacillus (bacterium) because it exists as inactive protoxins in them. But, once an insect ingests it, it is converted into an active form of toxin due to the alkaline pH of the alimentary canal. The activated toxin binds to the surface of midgut epithelial cells and creates pores that cause swelling and lysis and finally cause the death of the insect.

questions.text: The process of RNA interference (RNAi) has been used in the development of plants resistant to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The process of RNA interference has been used in the development of plants resistant to nematodes.

Fungi are generally decomposers, meaning they get their nourishment from decomposing or dead organic materials (usually plants). Insoluble polysaccharides, such as cellulose and lignin in dead wood, can be broken down by fungal exoenzymes into readily absorbable glucose molecules.

A virus is an infectious microorganism made up of a nucleic acid segment (DNA or RNA) encased in a protein coat. A virus cannot replicate on its own; instead, it must infect cells and use host cell components to replicate itself.

Any little creature has six legs and three segments to its body Insects frequently have wings as well.Insects include ants, bees, and flies.Any member of the largest class of the phylum Arthropoda, which is itself the largest of the animal phyla, is an insect (class Insecta or Hexapoda).

questions.explanation: Many nematodes live in plants and animals including human beings. A nematode Meloidogyne incognita infests the roots of tobacco plants and causes a great reduction in yield. A novel strategy was adopted to prevent this infection that was based on the process of RNA interference (RNAi). RNA interference (RNAi) is the phenomenon of inhibiting the activity of a gene by the synthesis of RNA molecules complementary to the mRNA. The normal (in vivo synthesized) mRNA of a gene is said to be “sense” because it carries the codons that are “read” during translation. Normally, the complement to the mRNA “sense” strand will not contain a sequence of codons that can be translated to produce a functional protein; thus, this complementary strand is called “anti-­sense RNA”. The anti­-sense RNA and mRNA molecules will anneal to form duplex RNA molecules (or double-stranded RNA) and the duplex RNA molecules can not be translated. Thus, the presence of anti­sense RNA will block translation of the mRNA of the affected gene.

questions.text: Which one of the following statements is correct regarding blood pressure?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A blood pressure reading of 130/90 mm Hg is classified as elevated, but it does not automatically necessitate treatment. Management may involve lifestyle changes, and treatment should be discussed with a healthcare provider.

This statement is misleading. While lower blood pressure can be associated with good health, 100/55 mm Hg might be too low for some individuals. Ideal blood pressure varies based on personal health factors.
Blood pressure readings do not determine a person's activity level. Activity can be influenced by various factors, and blood pressure can change during physical exertion.

options.text: 190/110 mm Hg may harm vital organs
options.explanation: A reading of 190/110 mm Hg indicates severe hypertension, which can lead to serious health issues, including damage to critical organs such as the brain and kidneys.

questions.explanation: Severe hypertension (≥180/110 mmHg) damages kidneys, brain, and heart, making it life-threatening.

questions.text: Arteries are best defined as the vessels which:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Carry blood away from the heart
options.explanation: Arteries are best defined as the vessels which carry blood away from the heart. They have thick walls and are elastic, which allows them to withstand the pressure of the blood being pumped by the heart. Arteries branch into smaller and smaller vessels as they get further away from the heart.

options.text: Break up into capillaries, then form veins
options.explanation: Arteries break up into capillaries, which are the smallest blood vessels in the body. Capillaries are so small that red blood cells can only pass through them one at a time. Capillaries allow the exchange of oxygen and nutrients between the blood and the tissues. The capillaries then reunite to form veins, which carry blood back to the heart.
Visceral arteries branch off of the abdominal aorta and supply blood to the stomach, intestines, liver, spleen, pancreas, and kidneys.

The arteries that supply oxygenated blood to different organs include the coronary arteries, pulmonary arteries, cerebral arteries, carotid arteries, subclavian arteries, abdominal aorta, and femoral arteries.

questions.explanation: Arteries are defined by their direction of flow—always carrying blood away from the heart, irrespective of oxygenation.

questions.text: Which plasma protein is involved in blood coagulation?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Albumin's main role is to maintain osmotic balance and transport substances in the bloodstream. It is not directly involved in blood coagulation.
Serum amylase is an enzyme that aids in the breakdown of carbohydrates. It has no role in the coagulation of blood.
Globulins are involved in immune functions and transport processes, but they do not play a critical role in blood clotting.
Fibrinogen is directly involved in blood coagulation. It is converted into fibrin to form a stable mesh that supports the blood clot.

questions.explanation: Fibrinogen is converted into fibrin during clotting, forming a mesh that stabilizes the blood clot.

questions.text: ‘Bundle of His’ is a part of which organ?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The brain is responsible for processing sensory information, controlling movements, and regulating cognitive functions, but it is not associated with the 'Bundle of His'.
The 'Bundle of His' is a critical component of the heart's electrical conduction system, ensuring the coordinated contraction of the heart chambers for effective blood circulation.
The kidney's main functions include filtering blood and regulating waste, fluid, and electrolytes, which are unrelated to the 'Bundle of His'.
The pancreas regulates insulin and digestive enzymes, which are unrelated to the heart's electrical conduction system and the 'Bundle of His'.

questions.explanation: The Bundle of His conducts impulses from the AV node to ventricles, ensuring synchronized contraction of the heart.

questions.text: Which one of the following human organs is often called the “graveyard” of RBCs?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The gall bladder is not known as the "graveyard" of RBCs. It stores bile produced by the liver and releases it into the small intestine to aid in digestion.
The kidney filters waste products and excess substances from the blood to form urine. It is not commonly referred to as the "graveyard" of RBCs.

The human organ that is often called the "graveyard" of red blood cells (RBCs) is the spleen. The spleen plays a role in removing old, damaged, or worn-out red blood cells from circulation. It also helps recycle components of RBCs, such as iron, for reuse in the body.

The liver is involved in various metabolic processes, including the synthesis and breakdown of molecules. While the liver can be involved in the breakdown of old blood cells, it is not commonly referred to as the "graveyard" of RBCs.

questions.explanation: The human organ that is often called the "graveyard" of red blood cells (RBCs) is the spleen. The spleen plays a role in removing old, damaged, or worn-out red blood cells from circulation. It also helps recycle components of RBCs, such as iron, for reuse in the body.

questions.text: The figure shows a human blood cell. Identify it and give its characteristics.
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Basophil - Involved in inflammatory response and allergic reactions
options.explanation: Basophils are white blood cells with large, irregular shapes and dark-staining granules in their cytoplasm. These granules contain histamine and other inflammatory mediators. Upon encountering an allergen, basophils release these substances, playing a key role in allergic reactions and inflammation.

options.text: B-lymphocyte - Key player in adaptive immune response, produces antibodies
options.explanation: B-lymphocytes are characterized by their large nuclei and minimal cytoplasm. They play a vital role in the adaptive immune system by producing antibodies in response to specific antigens, thus marking them for destruction by other immune cells.

options.text: Neutrophil - Most abundant white blood cell, essential for phagocytosis
options.explanation: Neutrophils have a multi-lobed nucleus and granular cytoplasm. They are the most prevalent white blood cells and are crucial for the innate immune response, acting as the first line of defense through phagocytosis of bacteria and fungi.

options.text: Monocyte - Large cells that differentiate into macrophages or dendritic cells
options.explanation: Monocytes are large cells with a kidney-shaped nucleus. They act as phagocytes and can differentiate into macrophages or dendritic cells in tissues, contributing to the body's immune defense by presenting antigens to T cells.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Basophil. Basophils are white blood cells that contain granules filled with histamine and other substances. They play a significant role in allergic reactions and inflammation by releasing these substances, which dilate blood vessels and increase vascular permeability. The other options are incorrect for the following reasons: B-lymphocytes are primarily involved in producing antibodies as part of the adaptive immune system, not in releasing inflammatory mediators. Neutrophils are phagocytic cells that are the most abundant white blood cells, focusing on engulfing pathogens rather than releasing histamine. Finally, Monocytes are large phagocytic cells that differentiate into macrophages or dendritic cells in tissues, and their primary function is not related to histamine release or immediate inflammatory responses.

questions.text: The figure shows blood circulation in humans with labels A to D. Select the option which gives correct identification of label and functions of the part.
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A: D-Capillary – Thin-walled, without a muscle layer, and wall one cell layer thick
options.explanation: Capillaries are indeed very thin-walled and allow for the exchange of substances between blood and tissues. However, the description is inaccurate regarding the number of cell layers. But in the images D is not capillaries as the B is capillaries

options.text: B: C-Vein – Thin-walled; blood flows smoothly
options.explanation: Veins are correctly described as thin-walled and facilitate the return of blood to the heart, but blood in veins does not flow in jerks/spurts as described.C → Vein(Thin-walled, wide lumen, carries blood towards the heart, often has valves)

options.text: C: D-Pulmonary vein – Carries oxygenated blood to the heart, pO2 = 95 mmHg
options.explanation: D → Lungs(Site of oxygenation of blood in pulmonary circulation)

options.text: D: A-Artery – Thick-walled; blood flows intermittently
options.explanation: D → Lungs(Site of oxygenation of blood in pulmonary circulation)

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C: D-Pulmonary vein – Carries oxygenated blood to the heart, pO2 = 95 mmHg. The pulmonary vein is responsible for transporting oxygen-rich blood from the lungs to the left atrium of the heart, which is consistent with its function and the provided pO2 value.Option A is incorrect because while capillaries are thin-walled, they are only one cell layer thick, not two as described.Option B is incorrect because veins do not carry blood in jerks/spurts; they have valves that help maintain a steady flow back to the heart.Option D is incorrect because arteries do carry blood from the heart, but the description of blood flow as 'evenly' is misleading; it should be 'in pulses' or 'intermittently' due to the heart's pumping action.

questions.text: What is vital capacity of our lungs?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Inspiratory reserve volume plus expiratory reserve volume
options.explanation: This option describes the combination of the inspiratory and expiratory reserve volumes, but it does not include the tidal volume necessary to calculate the full vital capacity. Therefore, it is not the complete calculation of vital capacity.

options.text: Total lung capacity minus residual volume
options.explanation: This is the correct option. The vital capacity is calculated by subtracting the residual volume (air that remains in the lungs after maximal exhalation) from the total lung capacity (the maximum amount of air the lungs can hold).

options.text: Inspiratory reserve volume plus tidal volume
options.explanation: This option incorrectly combines inspiratory reserve volume and tidal volume, which does not account for the expiratory reserve volume necessary to determine vital capacity. Thus, it is not an accurate method of calculation.

options.text: Total lung capacity minus expiratory reserve volume
options.explanation: This option incorrectly suggests subtracting the expiratory reserve volume from the total lung capacity, which does not account for the vital capacity. Vital capacity requires both inspiratory and expiratory reserve volumes.

questions.explanation: All options are explained below:A. Inspiratory reserve volume plus expiratory reserve volume:The vital capacity of the lungs is calculated by adding the inspiratory reserve volume (the maximum additional air that can be inhaled after a normal inhalation) to the expiratory reserve volume (the maximum additional air that can be exhaled after a normal exhalation). This option is accurate and describes the correct method for calculating vital capacity.B. Total lung capacity minus residual volume (Correct Option):: The vital capacity of the lungs is indeed calculated as the difference between the total lung capacity (the maximum amount of air the lungs can hold) and the residual volume (the volume of air that always remains in the lungs, even after forceful exhalation). This option is accurate, and it describes the correct calculation for vital capacity.C. Inspiratory reserve volume plus tidal volume: This option is not accurate. The vital capacity is not calculated by adding the inspiratory reserve volume to the tidal volume (the amount of air normally inhaled and exhaled during quiet breathing). Vital capacity includes the inspiratory and expiratory reserve volumes.D. Total lung capacity minus expiratory reserve volume: This option is not accurate. Subtracting the expiratory reserve volume from the total lung capacity does not yield the vital capacity. The vital capacity includes both the inspiratory and expiratory reserve volumes.Summary: The correct option for calculating the vital capacity of the lungs is B, which involves subtracting the residual volume from the total lung capacity. This method accurately represents the maximum amount of air that can be forcefully exhaled after a maximum inhalation. Option A, although accurate, combines the inspiratory and expiratory reserve volumes to calculate vital capacity. Options C and D do not correctly describe the calculation of vital capacity.

questions.text: What is true about RBCs in humans?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: They carry about 20­-25 percent of CO2
options.explanation: Red blood cells (RBCs) play a significant role in carrying carbon dioxide (CO2) in the blood. About 20-25 percent of the CO2 produced in the body is transported by RBCs, primarily in the form of bicarbonate ions and carbamino compounds.

While RBCs are responsible for transporting oxygen, they do not carry 99.5 percent of it. Approximately 97-98 percent of oxygen is bound to hemoglobin within RBCs, with a small fraction dissolved in plasma.

options.text: They transport about 80 percent oxygen only, and the rest 20 percent of it is transported in dissolved state in blood plasma
options.explanation: RBCs transport about 97-98 percent of oxygen. The remaining 2-3 percent is dissolved in the plasma, not 20 percent.

options.text: They do not carry CO2 at all.
options.explanation: RBCs do carry CO2, primarily in the form of bicarbonate ions and carbamino compounds. They are crucial for CO2 transport from tissues to the lungs.

questions.explanation: All options are explained below: a)They carry about 20-25 percent of CO2 (Correct Option): Red blood cells (RBCs) play a significant role in carrying and transporting carbon dioxide (CO2) in the blood. About 20-25 percent of the CO2 produced in the body is transported by RBCs. This transport occurs in multiple forms, including the conversion of CO2 to bicarbonate ions (HCO3-) within RBCs and the binding of CO2 to hemoglobin as carbamino compounds. RBCs help facilitate the removal of CO2 from body tissues to be transported to the lungs for exhalation.B. They transport 99.5 percent of O2: This statement is not accurate. While RBCs are responsible for transporting oxygen (O2) in the blood, they do not transport 99.5 percent of it. The majority of oxygen in the blood is carried by hemoglobin within the RBCs, but there is also some dissolved oxygen in the blood plasma.C. They transport about 80 percent oxygen only, and the rest 20 percent of it is transported in dissolved state in blood plasma:This statement is more accurate. RBCs are responsible for transporting approximately 97-98 percent of oxygen, while the remaining 2-3 percent is carried in a dissolved state in the blood plasma. The majority of oxygen binds to hemoglobin within RBCs.D. They do not carry CO2 at all:This statement is not accurate. RBCs do carry carbon dioxide (CO2), primarily in the form of bicarbonate ions (HCO3-) and carbamino compounds. They play a crucial role in CO2 transport from body tissues to the lungs for exhalation.Summary: The correct option (A) accurately describes the role of RBCs in transporting CO2. They carry about 20-25 percent of the CO2 produced in the body. In addition to CO2, RBCs also play a significant role in transporting oxygen (O2), with approximately 97-98 percent of O2 being carried by hemoglobin within RBCs, while a small percentage is dissolved in the blood plasma. The other options either inaccurately represent percentages or claim that RBCs do not participate in CO2 transport.

questions.text: The bulk of carbon dioxide (CO2 ) released from body tissues into the blood is present as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Bicarbonate in blood plasma and RBCs
options.explanation: Bicarbonate in blood plasma and RBCs (Correct Option):The bulk of carbon dioxide (CO2) released from body tissues into the blood is present as bicarbonate ions (HCO3-) in both blood plasma and red blood cells (RBCs). This process involves the conversion of CO2 to carbonic acid (H2CO3) and its subsequent dissociation into bicarbonate ions (HCO3-) and hydrogen ions (H+). This bicarbonate system plays a major role in transporting CO2 from body tissues to the lungs for elimination.

Free CO2 in blood plasma: While some CO2 is transported as free CO2 molecules in blood plasma, it is a relatively smaller proportion compared to the bicarbonate form. The majority of CO2 is carried as bicarbonate ions and carbamino compounds.

70% carbamino-hemoglobin and 30% as bicarbonate:This option presents an inaccurate distribution of CO2 forms. Carbamino-hemoglobin refers to CO2 bound to hemoglobin within red blood cells, which is a smaller fraction compared to bicarbonate transport. The majority of CO2 is transported as bicarbonate ions, while some is bound to hemoglobin.

Carbamino-hemoglobin in RBCs:While CO2 can bind to hemoglobin within red blood cells (carbamino-hemoglobin), it is not the primary form of CO2 transport. The primary method is the conversion of CO2 to bicarbonate ions, as explained in option A.

questions.explanation: Most of the carbon dioxide released from body tissues into the blood is transported as bicarbonate ions in both the plasma and red blood cells. A smaller amount binds with hemoglobin to form carbaminohemoglobin, and only a tiny fraction remains as free CO₂ in the plasma.

questions.text: Which one of these is the possibility for most of us with regard to breathing, by making a conscious effort?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: One can consciously breathe in and out using only the diaphragm without moving the ribs
options.explanation: This is correct. Conscious control can be exerted to use the diaphragm alone for breathing, a technique known as diaphragmatic breathing. This method is often employed in relaxation and meditation practices.

options.text: It is possible to completely empty the lungs by forcefully exhaling all air
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Although one can exhale most of the air forcefully, a residual volume of air always remains in the lungs to prevent their collapse and maintain structural integrity.

options.text: You can exhale all air from the lungs without any residual volume
options.explanation: This is inaccurate. The lungs retain a residual volume of air even after forceful exhalation, essential for keeping the lungs inflated and functional.

options.text: Breathing out through the Eustachian tubes is possible by closing both the nose and mouth
options.explanation: This is incorrect. The Eustachian tubes function to equalize ear pressure and are not involved in the respiratory process. Breathing through them is physiologically impossible.

questions.explanation: Conscious control can be exerted to use the diaphragm alone for breathing, a technique known as diaphragmatic breathing. This method is often employed in relaxation and meditation practices.

questions.text: A large proportion of oxygen remains unused in the human blood even after its uptake by the body tissues. This O2 _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Acts as a reserve during muscular exercise
options.explanation: This statement is correct. The unused oxygen in the blood acts as a reserve, which can be utilized during physical activities such as exercise when the body's oxygen demand increases. This ensures sufficient oxygen supply to the muscles.

options.text: Raises the pCO2 of blood to 75 mmHg
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Oxygen levels do not influence the partial pressure of carbon dioxide (pCO2) in the blood. pCO2 is regulated by cellular respiration and the elimination of CO2, independent of unused oxygen.

options.text: Is enough to keep oxyhaemoglobin saturation at 96%
options.explanation: This statement is misleading. Oxyhemoglobin saturation is maintained primarily by the normal binding of oxygen to hemoglobin, not solely by the presence of unused oxygen.

options.text: Helps in releasing more O2 to the epithelial tissues
options.explanation: This statement is inaccurate. Oxygen release to tissues is influenced by factors such as the oxygen gradient and hemoglobin affinity rather than directly by the presence of unused oxygen in the blood.

questions.explanation: The unused oxygen in the blood acts as a reserve, which can be utilized during physical activities such as exercise when the body's oxygen demand increases. This ensures sufficient oxygen supply to the muscles.

questions.text: The figure given below shows a small part of the human lung where exchange of gases takes place. Select the option that represents the labeled part (A, B, C, or D) correctly, along with its function:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Alveolar cavity - main site of exchange of respiratory gases
options.explanation: The alveolar cavity is indeed the primary site of gas exchange in the lungs. This is where oxygen is taken up by the blood from inhaled air, and carbon dioxide is released from the blood to be exhaled. The alveoli are tiny, thin-walled air sacs within the lungs, and they provide a large surface area for efficient gas exchange.

options.text: Red blood cells transport CO2 mainly
options.explanation: This statement is accurate. Red blood cells (RBCs) play a crucial role in the transport of carbon dioxide (CO2). They carry CO2 from body tissues to the lungs, where it can be exhaled.

options.text: Arterial capillary - passes oxygen to tissues
options.explanation: Arterial capillaries are part of the circulatory system and are responsible for delivering oxygen-rich blood to body tissues, not for "passing oxygen to tissues." options.text: Capillary wall - decrease surface area for exchange of O2 and CO2
options.explanation: Capillaries, including the capillary walls, serve the vital function of facilitating the exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide between the blood and the surrounding tissues. They do not decrease the surface area for gas exchange but rather maximize it.

questions.text: Which reaction will proceed in forward direction?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The value of Kc (equilibrium constant) determines the direction of a reaction. A Kc value greater than 1 indicates that the reaction favors the products, and the reaction will proceed in the forward direction.
A Kc value of 1 indicates that the reaction is at equilibrium, and there is no net forward or reverse reaction.
A Kc value less than 1 indicates that the reaction favors the reactants, and the reaction will proceed in the reverse direction.
A Kc value much less than 1 indicates that the reaction strongly favors the reactants, and the reaction will proceed in the reverse direction.

questions.explanation: The value of Kc (equilibrium constant) determines the direction of a reaction. A Kc value greater than 1 indicates that the reaction favors the products, and the reaction will proceed in the forward direction.

questions.text: Which pair of ions have five unpaired d-electrons?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Only Mn+2 has 5 unpaired electrons.
Only Mn+3 has 5 unpaired electrons.

questions.text: The color of transition metal complexes is due to transition of electrons between:
questions.subject: Chemistry

When transition metals form complex ions or compounds, the d orbitals split into two energy levels. Electrons from the lower energy level absorb light of wavelength corresponding to the visible region in the EM spectrum and get excited to a higher energy level. The remaining unabsorbed wavelength is reflected and that gives the characteristic colour to the complex. As the energy difference between the high and lower energy levels of electrons varies for different complexes, the colour also varies. questions.explanation: The color of transition metal complexes is due to the transition of electrons between different energy levels within the d orbitals of the metal ion. The d orbitals have different energy levels and can accommodate a varying number of electrons. When a transition metal ion forms a complex with ligands (atoms, ions, or molecules that surround the metal ion), the d orbitals split into different energy levels due to the influence of the ligands. This splitting is known as crystal field splitting. The energy difference between these split d orbitals corresponds to the energy of visible light. When white light passes through a colored solution containing transition metal complexes, certain wavelengths of light are absorbed by the electrons in the d orbitals, causing them to transition from a lower energy level to a higher energy level. The absorbed wavelengths are subtracted from the white light, resulting in the complementary color being observed. The specific color observed depends on the energy difference between the split d orbitals, which is influenced by factors such as the nature of the ligands and the oxidation state of the metal ion. Different ligands can induce different degrees of splitting, leading to variations in color. For example, complexes with ligands that create larger energy differences between the d orbitals tend to absorb longer wavelengths (lower energy) of light and appear yellow or red. Conversely, complexes with ligands that create smaller energy differences tend to absorb shorter wavelengths (higher energy) of light and appear blue or green. Overall, the absorption of specific wavelengths of light by transition metal complexes due to electron transitions within the d orbitals is responsible for their characteristic colors. options.explanation: Chloroethanoic acid (ClCH2COOH) is a strong acid among the choices due to the presence of the chlorine atom, which withdraws electron density and stabilizes the conjugate base (ClCH2COO-). questions.text: What is the reason of small atomic radii of sodium ions as compared to sodium atom?

The atomic radius of a sodium atom is 186 pm, while the ionic radius of a sodium ion (Na+) is 95 pm. The smaller radius of the sodium ion is due to the effective nuclear charge, which is the net positive charge experienced by the outermost electrons.When a sodium atom loses an electron to form a sodium ion, the number of electrons decreases, but the number of protons in the nucleus remains the same. This results in an increased effective nuclear charge, which pulls the remaining electrons closer to the nucleus, reducing the ionic radius.
Shielding effect refers to the reduction of the effective nuclear charge due to the presence of inner electrons, which is not applicable in this case.
Electron affinity is the energy change when an electron is added to an atom, which is not relevant to the radius of a sodium ion.

If force is doubled while velocity remains constant, power would increase by a factor of 2, not squared.

Doubling the force while keeping velocity constant would result in an increase in power. If force is doubled, the work done per unit time (power) would also double, assuming no change in velocity. Therefore, the power would increase, not halve.

questions.text: When the frequency of the applied force becomes equal to one of natural frequencies of body then the body oscillates with maximum displacement this phenomenon is called:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Heating refers to the process of increasing the temperature of an object or substance. It occurs when energy is transferred to the object, typically resulting in an increase in kinetic energy of its particles, leading to higher temperature. In the context of oscillations and resonance, heating may occur due to friction or other dissipative processes that convert mechanical energy into thermal energy.

Resonance is the phenomenon that occurs when the frequency of an external force applied to a system matches the natural frequency of the system. This results in the system oscillating with maximum amplitude, as energy is efficiently transferred to the system. Resonance can occur in various systems, including mechanical oscillators, electrical circuits, and acoustic systems. In mechanical systems, resonance can lead to structural vibrations or even structural failure if not properly damped.

Reverberation refers to the persistence of sound in an enclosed space due to repeated reflections off surfaces. It is commonly experienced in rooms with hard and reflective surfaces, such as concert halls or empty rooms. Reverberation can affect the clarity and quality of sound, and it is often controlled or mitigated in acoustic design to optimize the acoustic environment.

Damping is the process by which energy is dissipated or removed from a vibrating system, causing its amplitude to decrease over time. Damping can occur due to various factors such as friction, air resistance, or energy transfer to other modes of vibration. It is essential for controlling oscillations and preventing excessive vibration in mechanical systems. In the context of resonance, damping is necessary to prevent the system from oscillating uncontrollably at resonance frequencies.

questions.explanation: Resonance describes the phenomenon of increased amplitude that occurs when the frequency of a periodically applied force is equal or close to a natural frequency of the system on which it acts.

questions.text: Which of the following phenomenon proves that light waves are transverse waves?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Polarization refers to the orientation of oscillations in a transverse wave. When light waves are polarized, it means that the oscillations of the electric field (and magnetic field, in the case of electromagnetic waves) occur in a specific direction. For example, if light is polarized vertically, the electric field oscillates vertically.Polarization can occur when light interacts with certain materials, such as polarizing filters. These filters allow light waves oscillating in a particular direction to pass through while blocking light oscillating in other directions.The phenomenon of polarization demonstrates that light behaves as a transverse wave because its oscillations occur perpendicular to the direction of propagation.

Refraction is the bending of light as it passes from one medium to another with a different optical density. This bending occurs due to a change in the speed of light when transitioning between media.

Interference occurs when two or more light waves combine to form a resultant wave. This can result in constructive interference (where waves reinforce each other) or destructive interference (where waves cancel each other out).

Diffraction refers to the bending of waves around obstacles and the spreading of waves as they pass through narrow openings.When light encounters an obstacle or passes through a narrow slit, it bends around the edges, resulting in a diffraction pattern. This pattern is characteristic of wave behavior.

questions.explanation: Only transverse waves can undergo polarisation. Longitudinal waves cannot undergo polarization. Since light can undergo polarisation, it is a transverse wave.In summary, while refraction, interference, and diffraction are all phenomena that demonstrate the wave nature of light, they do not specifically prove that light waves are transverse waves. Each of these phenomena can be understood within the broader framework of wave optics, which encompasses both transverse and longitudinal waves.
